title: Skein Valued Counts of Open Curves
speaker: Tobias Ekholm
abstract: We show that skein valued counts of open holomorphic curves in a symplectic Calabi-Yau 3-fold with Maslov zero Lagrangian boundary condition are invariant under deformations and discuss applications (Ooguri-Vafa conjecture and simple recursion relations for the topological vertex). We then discuss the underlying polyfold perturbation scheme that allows for Gromov-Witten counts of bare curves, i.e. leaving area zero curves unperturbed, where the key ingredient is the observation that ghost bubbles leave traces on bare curves in combination with estimates on the failure of the existence of forgetful maps. The talk reports on joint work with V. Shende.

proof_stages.label: Calabi-Yau dimension-zero benchmark and closed cobordism
proof_stages.summary: The talk sets up a Calabi-Yau threefold with Maslov-zero Lagrangian data, recalls the virtual dimension formula giving rigid curves, and uses closed Gromov-Witten theory as the benchmark where one-parameter counts are invariant because nodal degenerations have real codimension two.

proof_stages.label: Codimension-one obstruction for open counts
proof_stages.summary: For curves with Lagrangian boundary the same dimension count suggests zero-dimensional moduli, but real boundary/nodal degenerations occur as codimension-one ends of one-parameter families, so a naive numerical open count cannot be invariant.

proof_stages.label: Boundary-node wall crossing and the HOMFLY skein relation
proof_stages.summary: Analyzing the first open degeneration, the nodal curve and the image of its normalization differ by one Euler characteristic, so imposing the HOMFLY skein relation with the z-variable cancels the jump in the count.

proof_stages.label: Linking, framing, and the skein-valued invariant
proof_stages.summary: The second degeneration changes the linking of the curve with the Lagrangian, leading to the a-variable, a framing relation, and a four-chain definition of linking; with these data the curve count takes values in the HOMFLY skein module and becomes invariant.

proof_stages.label: S^3 skein evaluation and conormal-knot conifold application
proof_stages.summary: Using the identification of the skein of S^3 with the HOMFLY polynomial, the shifted conormal of a knot in T^*S^3 gives a small cylinder counted by that polynomial, and SFT stretching plus the conifold transition moves the count to the resolved conifold where it becomes the Ooguri-Vafa holomorphic-curve count.

proof_stages.label: Bare curves and the zero-area ghost problem
proof_stages.summary: To make the skein wall-crossing argument compatible with perturbations, the construction avoids perturbing constant or zero-area components and counts bare curves, leaving the central compactness issue of excluding unwanted zero-area ghost bubbles.

proof_stages.label: Neck Fourier anti-bubbling criterion
proof_stages.summary: A Fourier expansion on a long neck shows that the first coefficient simultaneously records the derivative of the positive-area limit and the degree/Euler-characteristic information on the ghost side, so a ghost bubble would force a singularity in a previously treated curve; the Q&A adds that the non-integrable case is handled by a Carleman-similarity-type reduction.

proof_stages.label: Fast cutoffs and approximate forgetful maps in the perturbation scheme
proof_stages.summary: For perturbed equations the cutoff to zero perturbation is compressed so the relevant Fourier coefficient is barely changed, and an induction over Euler characteristic together with approximate forgetful maps and transversality gives compact solution-free neighborhoods and hence well-defined closed and open skein-valued counts.

examples.engagement_reason: the speaker analyzes the genus-two-to-genus-one-plus-ghost-bubble degeneration via neck/Fourier coefficient behavior and explains the derivative-vanishing trace it leaves

examples.name: Two zero-area pieces breaking a forgetful map
examples.note: A configuration with zero-area components that become unstable after forgetting marked data shows why perturbing constant components would make the images of the attaching points disagree and hence destroy exact forgetful maps.

examples.name: Two torus-attachment strata with the same glued domain
examples.note: The domain obtained by attaching two torus-like ghost pieces can arise from two nearby strata with slightly different perturbations, revealing the need to place cutoffs far enough out so the relevant sections are arbitrarily close.
examples.engagement_reason: the speaker compares two torus-attachment strata yielding the same glued domain and reasons through the induced perturbations, cutoff placement, and transversality consequences
